macro_segments.title: Ownership of Tree Roots Growing into Neighboring Property

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishnah: Roots Cut During Excavation
Yaakov Hadava's Question to Rav Chisda: Do Roots Belong to Tree Owner or Yard Owner?
Analysis of Baraita on Hekdesh Roots: Attempting to Prove Ownership Follows the Tree
Gemara's Challenge to the Hekdesh Proof: Both Cases Lack Me'ilah
Resolution: Growth After Sanctification Lacks Me'ilah Regardless of Location
Rav Papa's Distinction: Within vs. Outside Sixteen Amot

macro_segments.title: Ulla's Teaching on Tree Placement and Bikurim: Sixteen Amot of Nutrient Absorption

micro_segments.title: Ulla's Statement: Tree Within Sixteen Amot of Boundary Steals from Neighbor's Nutrients
Clarification: Gazlan Language Does Not Forbid Planting; Affects Bikurim Obligation
Source of Sixteen Amot: Mishnah on Saplings in Beit Se'ah
Calculation: Ten Saplings in 2500 Amot = 250 Amot per Tree (Doesn't Match 16 Amot)
Gemara's Imprecision Challenge: Can Ulla Round Up If Result Exempts from Bikurim?
Square vs. Circle: Moving from 32×32 to Circular Radius of 16 Amot
Pi Calculation: Gemara Uses Pi=3; Square Exceeds Circle by One Quarter
Circle Area: 16² × 3 = 768 Amot (vs. Mishnah's 833 Amot for Three Trees)
Resolution: Ulla Meant 16⅔ Amot, Not Exactly 16; Rounding Up Is Stringent (Lechumra)

macro_segments.title: Challenges to Ulla's Sixteen Amot Rule from Mishnayot on Land and Bikurim

micro_segments.title: Buying Tree with Land: Why Mishnah Says Small Amount Suffices (Against 16 Amot Requirement)
Buying Two vs. Three Trees: Automatic Land Transfer Requires Three Trees, Not 16 Amot
Rabbi Akiva: Even Tiny Land Obligates in Pe'ah, Bikurim, and Prosbol (Against 16 Amot)
Gemara Distinguishes: Akiva Discusses Wheat/Grain, Not Trees; Only Trees Need 16 Amot

macro_segments.title: Tree at Boundary Between Eretz Yisrael and Chutz La'aretz: Bikurim and Nutrient-Based Ownership

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: Fruit from Split Tree—Is It Mixed Tevel/Chulin or Divided by Boundary?
Rabbi vs. Rabban Shimon: Does Nutrient Flow Unite Tree or Does Boundary Split It?
Gemara: Debate Is Breira Doctrine; Special Case Assumes Rock Divides Roots
Explanation: Rabbi Says Nutrients Mix in Trunk; Rabban Shimon Says Nutrients Stay Separated

macro_segments.title: Sixteen Amot vs. Twenty-Five Amot: Scope of Root Reach and Nutrient Absorption

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: Trees Must Be 25 Amot from Wells; Doesn't Ulla's 16 Amot Contradict This?
Abaye: Roots Go Far (to 25 Amot), But Only Weaken Ground (Draw Nutrients) Up to 16 Amot

macro_segments.title: Palestinian Amoraim: Reish Lakish vs. Rabbi Yochanan on Bikurim and Nutrient-Stealing

micro_segments.title: Reish Lakish's Question: Is Tree Near Boundary Gazlan Preventing Bikurim?
Rabbi Yochanan's Answer (Rav Dimi's Report): Gazlan—Cannot Bring Bikurim
Later Report (Ravin): Rabbi Yochanan Reverses—Tree Near or Overhanging Boundary Allows Bikurim
Reason: 'Al Menat Ken'—Joshua Apportioned Land Assuming Such Nutrient-Sharing Is Normal and Permitted

macro_segments.title: Mishnah: Cutting Overhanging Branches to Allow Plowing and Passage

micro_segments.title: Tree Overhanging Neighbor's Field: Cut to Height of Plow-Prod (Mardea) to Allow Plowing
Carob and Sycamore: Cut Entire Overhang Opposite Plumb Line Due to Heavy Shade
Irrigated Field (Beit Hashlachin): Cut Any Tree's Entire Overhang (Shade Harmful)
Abba Shaul: Any Non-Fruit Tree Cut Entire Overhang (Even Non-Irrigated)

macro_segments.title: Gemara on Branch Cutting: Abba Shaul's Position and Textual Evidence

micro_segments.title: Question: Does Abba Shaul Argue on First Case (Non-Irrigated) or Second Case (Irrigated)?
Baraita Shows Abba Shaul Explicitly Allows All Trees for Irrigated Field
Conclusion: Abba Shaul Argues on Non-Irrigated Case; Makes Distinction Between Fruit and Non-Fruit Trees
Rav Ashi: Mishnah Text Confirms via Word 'Kol' (All)—Expanding Tanna Kamma's Rule

macro_segments.title: Mishnah and Gemara: Tree Overhanging Public Domain (Rashut Harabim)

micro_segments.title: Tree Overhanging Rashut Harabim: Cut to Height Allowing Camel and Rider to Pass
Rabbi Yehuda: Measure by Camel Laden with Flax, Not Just Rider Height
Rabbi Shimon: Cut Entire Overhang Opposite Plumb Line Due to Tumah (Impurity)

macro_segments.title: Gemara on Rashut Harabim Branch Cutting: Current Assessment vs. Future Deterioration

micro_segments.title: Question: Why Care Only About Current Situation If Branches Will Regrow?
Answer: Go by Current Assessment; Mishnah Follows Rabbi Eliezer (Regular Maintenance Allowed)
Rabbi Yochanan: Unlike Tunnel Case, Branches Can Be Regularly Pruned Without Hidden Danger

macro_segments.title: Comparative Analysis: Rabbi Yehuda's Flax Height vs. Rabbanan's Person Height

micro_segments.title: Question: Which Is Higher—Person on Camel or Flax Load on Camel?
Logical Necessity: Flax Height Must Be Higher; Otherwise Rabbanan's Person-Height Fails for Flax Cases
Rabbi Yehuda's Accommodation: Person Can Duck; Flax Load Cannot—Only Flax Height Matters

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon's Tumah Rule: Entire Overhang Due to Ritual Impurity Concern

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon: Cut All Overhang Opposite Plumb Line to Prevent Tumah from Dead Bodies
Clarification: Not Just About Corpse Dropped on Branches; Rather Tent Effect (Ohel) of Overhang
Proof: If Only Dropped-On-Branch Concern, Pruning (Thinning) Would Suffice; Complete Removal Required Means Ohel Concern

topical_tags.term: תרומות ומעשרות (Terumot u'Ma'asrot / Priestly Gifts and Tithes)
טבל וחולין (Tevel v'Chulin / Untithed and Tithed Produce)
ארץ ישראל וחוץ לארץ (Eretz Yisrael and Chutz La'aretz)
על מנת כן (Al Menat Ken / With That Understanding/Condition)
אדמה אשר נתתה לי (Adamah Asher Natata Li / The Land That You Gave Me)
